title: Provide breadcrumbs for interfaces deeper than two levels
summary: Generated applications with multi-level information architecture (settings, account management, documentation, file browsers) omit breadcrumbs, forcing users to rely on browser back-button or guess parent-page URLs to navigate back up the hierarchy.
instruction: Any page that represents the third or deeper level in a content hierarchy must provide a visible breadcrumb trail showing at least the path from the top level to the current page. The current page label should be the last breadcrumb item (non-interactive), and each ancestor must be a clickable link back to that level.
rationale: Breadcrumbs reduce navigation errors in deep hierarchies by 10-15% (Nielsen Norman Group). Without them, users navigating to a third-level page have no way to jump back to the first level or any intermediate level — they must use the browser's back button (which may exit the site entirely) or guess URLs.
violations: A settings page at /account/settings/notifications/preferences with no breadcrumbs — the user must click 'Back to Settings' through three separate back-navigation clicks.
A documentation site with a five-level sidebar hierarchy but no breadcrumb trail on article pages — users in the third+ level cannot tell where they are in the IA.
A file management interface where opening a deeply nested folder shows no path — the user cannot navigate back to the root without manually clicking the folder hierarchy in the sidebar.
controls: A settings page with breadcrumb: Account > Settings > Notifications > Preferences (last item non-link, bold). Each ancestor is a clickable link.
A documentation site with breadcrumb: Docs > Section > Article, displayed below the header with a small separator icon and reduced font-size.
A file manager with a clickable path: Home > Projects > Design > Assets (each segment is a link, current segment is bold text).
borderlineCases: A single-level microsite (one landing page with anchor links to sections) — there is no hierarchy to navigate.
contextualExceptions: Application interfaces where the primary navigation is a sidebar/tree view that always shows the full path (the sidebar itself serves the breadcrumb function).
Wizard or multi-step flows where a step indicator substitutes for breadcrumb hierarchy.
Mobile-first designs where breadcrumbs are collapsed into a 'Back' button and page title due to limited horizontal space.
detectorSignal: Pages with URL depth >= 3 (three or more path segments) that lack any element with role=navigation, aria-label=breadcrumb, or class containing 'breadcrumb'
matcherProposal: Parse the URL path. If path segments (excluding domain) >= 3, check the rendered DOM for: (a) element with aria-label='breadcrumb', (b) element with role='navigation' and 'breadcrumb' in class or label, (c) an ordered list of links before the main content heading. Flag if none found.
detectorLimitations: Cannot detect breadcrumbs rendered purely with JavaScript (SPA dynamic content) without waiting for full app bootstrap.
Some applications use a 'Back' button + location indicator instead of breadcrumbs — functionally similar but structurally different.
Flat IA where deep URLs are used for SEO (URL structure does not match user-visible hierarchy) — breadcrumbs may be unnecessary.
falsePositiveScenarios: An application with a persistent sidebar tree that shows the full navigation path — breadcrumbs are redundant despite a deep URL.
A wizard or checkout flow where the user is guided linearly and doesn't need to navigate back to arbitrary ancestors.
A flat-hierarchy site where SEO-friendly deep URLs (e.g., /blog/2024/06/article-title) don't correspond to a three-level user-facing hierarchy.
mitigationStrategy: Only flag when URL depth >= 3 AND the page is not part of a wizard/multi-step flow (check for step indicators) AND no persistent navigation tree is present on the page. Lower to warning for flat-hierarchy deep URLs.

fixturePlans.violation: A settings page at /account/preferences/notifications/critical — four URL segments, no breadcrumb, no sidebar tree, user must rely on browser back button.
fixturePlans.control: The same settings page with breadcrumb: 'Account > Preferences > Notifications > Critical' with clickable ancestors and bold current-page label.
fixturePlans.borderline: A checkout flow at /checkout/shipping/payment/confirm with a step indicator showing 'Step 3 of 4' — no breadcrumbs, but the step indicator serves the orientation function.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
Breadcrumb usability statistics based on publicly published Nielsen Norman Group research.
overlapNotes: Second navigation proposal. Distinct from P-A-020 (active state ambiguity). No overlap with existing rules.
